videos.title: Best Things To Do in Kuala Lumpur Malaysia 4K
videos.description:
Are you looking for the best things to do in Kuala Lumpur? We just created a list of 30 must do activities for you to do while visiting Kuala Lumpur Malaysia! In this Kuala Lumpur travel guide we show you around Bukit Bintang, Jalon Alor, Genting Highlands, Chinatown and many other top tourist destinations in Kuala Lumpur.  Some of those Kuala Lumpur attractions include Petronas Towers, KLCC, Changkat, Thean Hou Temple, Merdeka Square, Self Care Massage, Sunway Lagoon Waterpark, Batu Caves, Menera Tower, Central Market, KL Bird Park and much more!  If you enjoy this Kuala Lumpur travel video you can also watch our other travel vlogs from across Malaysia.

It took me 5 days to make this Kuala Lumpur travel video. I would say you can explore most of Kuala Lumpur around Bukit Bintang, Chinatown, Batu Caves and the KLCC area in 3 days. You can easily do these attractions on your own and don't need a guide, just use Grab App to get around. I would suggest getting the city tour and the Metro Card for first time visitors. You can find the best Kuala Lumpur hotels in Bukit Bintang, Sentral and KLCC. The best food street in Kuala Lumpur is Jalan Alor.

videos.transcript: What's up guys, we're here in Kuala Lumpur. It's the vibrant capital of Malaysia, and in this video we're going to show you the best things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Kuala Lumpur is located in the west of Peninsular Malaysia. It's the bustling capital city and a true melting pot of cultures, with the majority of the population being Malay, Indian, and Chinese. For this reason, Kuala Lumpur enjoys incredible diversity, which can be seen in its delicious cuisine, iconic landmarks, and modern skyscrapers mixed with colonial architecture. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in Kuala Lumpur. Make sure to watch until the end because we also share three travel tips that will be helpful for your own trip. Now let's dive in! We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. One of the most popular things to do in Kuala Lumpur is to visit the impressive Batu Caves, which can be found just north of the city. This series of caves are tucked within a limestone cliff and houses several Hindu temples and statues. The Temple Cave is the main one, sitting at the top of a 272-step rainbow-colored staircase. At the entrance, you can find a giant golden statue of Lord Murugan, the Hindu God of War. The Temple Cave is free to visit but if you want to visit the other caves on site, you'll have to pay a small fee. Thanks to its blend of cultures, Kuala Lumpur offers a wide variety of food. Right in the heart of the city, you can find Jalan Alor, a street of atmospheric hawker stalls and restaurants. It's a great place to try some local food at affordable prices, with choices of barbecued meats, noodles, fried rice, juices, and lots of desserts. But if you're not familiar with Malaysian cuisine yet, we recommend going on a food tour. It's a perfect way to sample multiple dishes and snacks all while learning about the history of Malaysian cuisine. You can find a food tour Link in the description below the video. For a true escape of the hustle and bustle of the city, head to one of the beautiful parks. KL Forest Eco Park is one of the only remaining parts of a tropical rainforest within the city limits, featuring nature trails and canopy walks. It's a beautiful place to see flora species as well as local wildlife and birds. And you can easily combine your visit with the KL Tower, an important architectural marker soaring above the park. It's incredible how lush a park can be in the middle of the city. But definitely bring mosquito spray, because there's tons of bugs out here. To gain a new appreciation of Kuala Lumpur, we recommend taking in the skyline view from above. There are lots of rooftop bars in the city, ranging from classy restaurants to cocktail lounges. We visited Vertigo Bar, located at the 59th floor of the Banyan Tree. It offers a great panorama of the most iconic buildings in the city: the Petronas Twin Towers and KL Tower. Alternatively, you can head to the Sky Bar at Traders Hotel or the KL Tower Observation Deck. The jewel in the skyline of Kuala Lumpur are undoubtedly the Petronas Twin Towers. At a whopping 88 stories, these are the tallest Twin Towers in the world. At the ground level, be sure to check out Suria KLCC, a shopping center with designer stores, cafés, and restaurants. There's also a concert hall, art gallery, and the Discovery Center. At the top, there's an Observatory with views of the city and tickets also include a visit to the Sky Bridge that connects both towers. The downtown area of Kuala Lumpur is home to several interesting neighborhoods. Bukit Bintang is the entertainment epicenter and comes alive at night. Changkat, a stretch of bars and restaurants in Bukit Bintang, is a party haven for locals, tourists, and expats. From Irish bars and nightclubs to Cuban lounges and live music, there's a certain energy here that you can only really experience firsthand. But don't expect cheap alcohol prices. Another great area that you can't miss in the center of Kuala Lumpur is Chinatown. No matter what time of day it is, it's always bustling with people and Petaling Street Market is the heart of it all. This pedestrian-only street is filled with dozens of restaurants and food stalls. Other interesting places to visit in Chinatown include the oldest Taoist Temple in the city and Kwai Chai Hong, a restored 1960s Chinatown lane with stunning murals. So on this street you can find some really awesome street art, and it kind of reminds us of Georgetown in Penang, which we visited a couple weeks ago. Just like a miniature version. Another great way to experience Chinese culture in Kuala Lumpur is by visiting the Thean Hou Temple. This six-tiered Temple is one of the biggest in all of Southeast Asia, combining elements of Buddhism, Taoism, and Confucianism. Located on a hill a bit outside the city center, the temple also provides some fantastic views of Kuala Lumpur. Come at sunset to see the temple illuminated with hanging lanterns. The Cultural Bazaar, formerly known as the Central Market, is an important historic landmark in the city. Originally constructed in 1888 and 1889, this market served Kuala Lumpur's large tin mining community, selling fresh meat, fish, and produce. Today, it's a popular market for handicrafts, coffee stalls, and all sorts of delicious sweets. As it's a short walk away from Chinatown, Merdeka Square, and the oldest standing mosque in Kuala Lumpur, it's best to combine these activities. Finally, right next to the Petronas Twin Towers you can find the beautiful 50-acre KLCC Park, with lots of walking trails. This area is a water playground during the day and Lake Symphony show at night. And from this park, you'll have some of the best views of the Petronas Towers. Every evening, Lake Symphony hosts a light show featuring music, lights, water jets, and a giant fountain. The show only lasts about 10 minutes and usually starts at 8, 9, and 10 PM. As we promised at the beginning of our video, we'll now share three things that we learned in Kuala Lumpur that will help you on your own trip. First, Kuala Lumpur may seem like a big city on a map, but most of the touristic highlights are found in the same vicinity, so you can fit a lot of the sights into a short time. Ideally, spend two to three days in the city and stay in the heart of the downtown for easiest access. We've included a few great places to stay in the description below the video. Second, if you want to cut down on walking or simply cool down, take the air conditioned Bukit Bintang KLCC walkway, the LRT/monorail, or hop on the purple MyRapid buses to get around which are free of charge. There are also lots of Grab rides available at very affordable prices, so make sure to download the App before arriving. Finally, as Malaysia is situated right on the equator, the weather in Kuala Lumpur is mostly tropical, humid, and hot. Pack your most durable and breathable summer clothes to accommodate the temperatures. Rain is more likely to occur closer to the evenings, so make the best of your hot afternoons. There are also a few other things to do in Kuala Lumpur that we didn't mention and we've included a list of those on the screen here for you. If you're planning your Malaysia trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code below. We also included important links in the description that will help you with your Kuala Lumpur trip, such as an interactive map to get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

In this Malaysia travel guide we talk about Malaysia travel tips, cost of travel, hotels in Malaysia, Kuala Lumpur, Penang, Langkawi and much more. We talk about safety in Malaysia, currency, transportation, arrival process and more. The population of Malaysia is 35 million people and the official language is Malay. Most of the people of Malaysia are Malay, Chinese or Indian Hindu. When considering a trip to Malaysia many people originate in Kuala Lumpur then go to cities like Malacca, Kota Kinabulu, Penang or other islands. Malaysia has 879 islands and shares the island of Borneo with Indonesia. Malaysia has Sabah and Sarawak on Borneo and shares a border with Brunei. With talk about Malaysian food, the history of Malaysia and share relevant travel tips for first time visitors as well as seasoned travelers. 

This is my 5th time to visit Malaysia and every time I go I discover new places to visit. Malaysia is affordable and relatively safe from my experience.
